column2: Directory of providers about the data

The data here underpins the Find a Legal Advisor tool (FALA). Use this service to find legal aid advisers in England and Wales. They, (FALA), will ask about your problem and finances to work out if you can get legal aid.

Civil legal aid is divided into categories of law that cover different types of case. Civil legal aid providers will be authorised to carry out work in one or more category of law. The rows below set out some information on the sort of cases that are included in each category of law. Full descriptions of the categories of law can be found in the Category Definitions 2024 document on our website:
Please see below a brief description of each category
Legal Aid for damages claims against public authorities that can detain, imprison or prosecute, such as the police or prison. Also includes legal aid for damages claims in abuse in care cases.
Legal aid for clinical negligence cases where a baby has brain or nerve damage caused during pregnancy, childbirth or up to 8 weeks old.

Legal aid for problems getting the local authority or council to provide or pay for the right care. For carers, children, young people and adults. Also covers help if someone cannot make decisions about their health, day-to-day life or care due to their mental capacity
Legal aid for cases where you are at risk of losing your home due to failing to pay your mortgage
Legal aid for cases where you have been treated unfairly because of disabilities or health conditions, race, age, sex, religion or pregnancy.
Legal aid for certain education problems. Includes help if your child has Special Educational Needs and cases about discrimination in the provision of education.
Legal aid for mediation between two individuals in family matters

Legal aid for issues around housing, homelessness, and losing your home. Includes being evicted or forced to sell your home. Also covers problems with landlords, repairs, neighbours or council housing.
A scheme that provides free legal advice and representation to people facing eviction, including on-the-day advice at court.
Legal aid for help if you’re seeking asylum. Help to stay in the UK if you are a victim of modern slavery or human trafficking

Legal aid for issues that arise out of a family relationship. Includes advice about children in care, children and social services. Also, help with children and finances if you divorce or split up. In addition, cover advice on domestic abuse, including controlling behaviour, emotional abuse, or if someone is harassing, threatening or hurting you or a child.

Legal aid for mental health and mental capacity issues. This includes help if someone cannot make decisions about their health, day-to-day life or care. Also include help if you’re held in hospital (‘sectioned’), mental health tribunals and community treatment orders.
Legal aid for help if you’re a victim of human trafficking or modern slavery
Legal aid to bring legal action against public bodies. Includes police, government, prisons, NHS, the council, care facilities and schools.
Legal aid to appeal a decision about your benefits. Only covers appeals to the Upper Tribunal or beyond.
The name of the legal advisor, please note that this can differ from individual office names.
Highlights that an organisation is classed as a "Not For Profit". These organisation offer legal aid services without aiming to make a profit from the legal work done.

Civil legal aid is divided into categories of law that cover different types of case. Civil legal aid providers will be authorised to carry out work in one of more category of law. Full descriptions of the categories of law can be found in the Category Definitions 2024 document on our website:

The LAA commissions legal aid for each civil category of law using geographical regions known as Procurement Areas. Providers are allocated a specific number of contracted cases that they can deliver from a Procurement Area, which can be increased in certain circumstances. The Procurement Areas differ between categories with some being set at local authority level while others cover a larger area.

In legal aid, "matter starts" are the number of Controlled Work cases that a legal aid provider is allocated to open from that office each year. The number of matter starts allocated to a provider depends on the contract and the category of law and can be increased in certain circumstances.
The name of the legal advisor, please note that this can differ from individual office names.
Highlights that an organisation is classed as a "Not For Profit". These organisation offer legal aid services without aiming to make a profit from the legal work done.

Civil legal aid is divided into categories of law that cover different types of case. Civil legal aid providers will be authorised to carry out work in one of more category of law. While modern slavery is not a  category of law, this spreadsheet shows the providers that have been allocated additional cases to carry out this work.

In legal aid, "matter starts" are the number of Controlled Work cases that a legal aid provider is allocated to open from that office each year. This shows the number of matter starts these providers have available for (non-immigration) modern slavery cases.
The name of the legal advisor, please note that this can differ from individual office names.

HLPAS started on August 1, 2023, across England and Wales. It replaced the Housing Possession Court Duty Schemes (HPCDS). 

HLPAS provides “on-the-day” emergency advice and advocacy to anyone facing possession proceedings. Anyone in danger of eviction or having property repossessed can get free legal advice and representation at court on the day of their listed hearing. It also provides free legal advice (Early Legal Advice) on Housing, Debt and Welfare Benefits issues to individuals with evidence showing they are at risk of possession proceedings, loss of their home or illegal eviction.

column3: In court acts of assistance allocation
column4:
HLPAS In Court Duty Schemes provide “on-the-day” emergency advice and advocacy to anyone facing possession proceedings. Anyone in danger of eviction or having property repossessed can get free legal advice and representation at court on the day of their listed hearing.

In legal aid, "matter starts" (or ‘acts of assistance’ for HLPAS in court duty) are the number of cases that a legal aid provider is allocated to open. The number of matter starts allocated to a provider depends on the contract and the category of law

column3: Early Legal Advice (ELA) new matter starts Allocation
column4:
Early legal advice on housing, Debt, and Welfare benefits issues I available for individuals with evidence that they are at risk of possession proceedings, loss of their home or illegal eviction. It helps people resolve legal issues before they go to court.

In legal aid, "matter starts" are the number of cases that a legal aid provider is allocated to open. The number of matter starts allocated to a provider depends on the contract and the category of law.

The class of work which allows contracted providers to provide advice and assistance to detained clients at the police station and to provide representation at the magistrates court. It is the requirement to be able to access duty solicitor schemes in the police station and the magistrates court

The class of work that allows contracted providers to undertake work in matters arising from conviction and sentencing. The class of work is automatically bundled with Investigations & Proceedings and Prison Law. It is also available as a stand alone class
The class of work that allows a contracted legal advisor to provide help and assistance in relation to matters arising as result of  serving a prison sentence.
